title: Kitty Tree
summary: A tall, open cat tower framed from 2x2s with plywood platforms and ramps between levels — a floor-to-ceiling climbing structure the cat actually uses.
description:
This is a freeform cat tower rather than a precise plan, and it's better for it: cats want levels, landings, and a way up and down that isn't a leap, so you build to the cat and the corner you're putting it in, not to a fixed cut list. The source builder made theirs about 5-1/2 feet tall to reach a route over from the stairs — yours should suit your ceiling and your cat's confidence.

The structure is a simple post-and-shelf frame: 2x2 uprights tied together by 2x2 shelf supports, with 1/2" plywood dropped in as platforms and ramps. Two details from the build worth keeping. First, offset each pocket hole slightly from the center of its support so opposing screws don't collide inside the joint. Second, the platforms need to stick out past the frame so the cat has a landing to turn around on where a ramp meets a level — think of it as the landing on a staircase.

The ramps can be a touch steep for a cautious cat, so add 'speed bumps' — 1x2 offcuts screwed across the ramp — to give claws something to brace against. Carpet or sisal the platforms and ramps for grip and scratching. And because it's tall, anchor it: fix the top to a wall or wedge it floor-to-ceiling so it can't tip when the cat launches off it.

tools.note: The frame is 2x2s joined with pocket screws — offset each hole so opposing screws miss each other.
Crosscutting 2x2s and breaking down the plywood sheet.
For rounding platform corners or shaping ramps.
Every edge a cat brushes should be smooth and splinter-free.
Fast way to wrap platforms and ramps in carpet.

materials.name: 2x2 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Uprights and shelf supports. Exact count depends on height and level count.

materials.name: Carpet or sisal remnant
materials.note: Grip and scratching surface for platforms and ramps.

materials.name: 1x2 offcuts for ramp cleats
materials.note: Screwed across ramps as 'speed bumps' so claws can brace.

materials.name: Wall anchor bracket
materials.note: To fix the top to a wall stud so the tower can't tip.

cutList.note: ~21" for a roughly 2 ft square footprint; adjust to your design.
~66" gives a 5-1/2 ft tower; set to suit your ceiling.
Let them overhang the frame where a ramp lands so the cat has a landing.
Length set by the rise between levels.

steps.title: Cut and drill the shelf supports
steps.body: Cut the 2x2 shelf supports to your chosen footprint — about 21" gives a roughly 2 ft square. Put a pocket hole at each end, but offset each hole from the center of the support: because supports meet from all four sides, centered holes would drive screws into each other. Right-of-center on every one keeps them clear.

steps.title: Cut the uprights and lay out the levels
steps.body: Cut the four uprights to your target height — the source went ~5-1/2 ft to reach a route off the stairs; match your ceiling and cat. Mark the level heights on each upright now, identically, so the platforms end up dead level. Drill pocket holes in the bottoms of the uprights to fasten down to the base platform.

steps.title: Assemble the frame
steps.body: This is the fiddly part, especially solo — build the frame up one side at a time, fastening supports between uprights at your marked heights. Work on a flat floor and check the frame stands square and doesn't rack before you commit every screw. Fasten the base first to give the uprights something to stand on.

steps.title: Add platforms and ramps
steps.body: Cut plywood platforms and drop them onto the shelf supports, letting them overhang the frame where a ramp will land — that overhang is the landing the cat turns around on. Cut ramps to span between levels. All ramps running the same direction keeps the tower tight against the wall, which is usually what you want.

steps.title: Speed bumps, carpet, and anchoring
steps.body: Screw 1x2 offcuts across the ramps as grip cleats — steep ramps are hard on a cautious cat without them. Sand every edge, then wrap platforms and ramps in carpet or sisal for traction and scratching. Finally, anchor the top to a wall stud or wedge the tower floor-to-ceiling so it can't tip when the cat jumps off the top.

images.alt: Tall homemade cat tower with plywood platforms and ramps between levels
